title: Article 1.1 - What Is an Economy and Why Does It Matter to You?
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series

When you hear the word “economy,” you might picture central bank governors, complicated charts, and language that feels designed to confuse. In reality, an economy is just the sum of every transaction happening around you — the chapati you bought this morning, the farmer who grew the wheat, the bank that lent the farmer money, and the government that built the road the wheat travelled on.

For an investor, understanding the economy is not optional. The economy is the tide that lifts or sinks all boats. If the tide is rising, even a modest company can grow. If the tide is falling, even a great company can struggle. This article explains what an economy is, how it’s measured, and most importantly, how you can use that knowledge to make better decisions with your money.
1. The Economy: A Simple Definition
An economy is a system where people produce, distribute, and consume goods and services. In Tanzania, that system includes:
Households like yours — you work, earn, spend, and save.
Businesses — from a small kiosk in Kariakoo to CRDB Bank and TBL.
The Government — which builds roads, ports, and hospitals, and sets rules for business.
The Rest of the World — because we export gold, cashews, and tourism, and import machinery, oil, and medicine.
When all these parts are moving smoothly, the economy grows. When one part stalls — say, drought hits farmers or global prices rise — the effects ripple through every sector.
2. How Do We Know If the Economy Is Growing?

Economists use a measure called Gross Domestic Product (GDP). GDP is the total value of all goods and services produced inside Tanzania in one year. If GDP is higher than last year, the economy is expanding. If it’s lower, the economy is contracting.

For investors, GDP growth matters because it correlates with corporate profits. A growing economy means more people have jobs, more customers buy products, and more businesses earn profits. More profits eventually translate into higher share prices and larger dividends.

Tanzania in focus: In the most recent data, Tanzania’s economy has been growing at roughly 5–6% per year — one of the faster rates in East Africa. This growth is driven by construction, agriculture, mining, and financial services. When you see the Banking, Finance & Investment index on the DSE rising, that is not an accident — it reflects an economy where more people are borrowing, saving, and transacting.
3. What Are the Main Drivers of Tanzania’s Economy?
To invest intelligently, you need to know which engines power the country. These are the sectors that tend to create the most listed-company profits:
When the Daily DSE Wrap notes that the banking index is up, the root cause is often found in one of these engines performing well.
4. The Economy and Your Investment Portfolio
Here is the practical connection: the economy sets the environment in which every company operates. Your job as an investor is to ask:
Is the economy growing fast enough to lift my chosen companies?
Are there headwinds — like drought, high inflation, or political uncertainty — that could hurt earnings?
Which sectors stand to gain the most from where we are in the economic cycle?

Consider this scenario: The Bank of Tanzania lowers the benchmark interest rate. Borrowing becomes cheaper. Businesses expand, consumers spend more, and bank loan books grow. In response, bank share prices often rise because investors anticipate higher future profits. This is exactly the kind of analysis we do in our Daily DSE Wrap when we discuss the performance of the banking sector.
5. How to Track the Economy Yourself
You don’t need a PhD. You need three simple habits:
Check GDP growth figures when they are published (quarterly by NBS Tanzania). Is the trend up or down?
Monitor the Bank of Tanzania’s announcements — interest rate decisions, inflation reports, and monetary policy statements. We cover key ones in the Daily Wrap.
Watch commodity prices (gold, oil, maize). They directly affect Tanzania’s trade balance and the shilling.
Combine these with the market data we provide every trading day, and you will begin to see patterns — for example, that a falling interest rate often precedes a rally in banking stocks.
6. From Macro to Market: The Daily DSE Wrap Connection

In every Daily DSE Wrap, we start with the Market Pulse — a snapshot of the day’s turnover, indices, and foreign flow. That snapshot is the heartbeat of the Tanzanian economy as expressed through its listed companies. When we say “the banking sector led the market higher,” the reason is embedded in the economic story you now understand.
Next time you read the Daily Wrap, ask yourself: What is the economy doing that might explain this move?
If you want to go deeper into how to analyse a specific banking stock, proceed to our article (Module 4.3).
Key Takeaway

An economy is not a mysterious force. It is the total of millions of everyday transactions. By understanding its direction, you gain a powerful lens through which to view every company, every sector, and every market movement. The economy provides the context; the Daily DSE Wrap provides the daily translation.
Put It Into Practice

Open today’s Daily DSE Wrap and identify one piece of data that you can now explain using the economic concepts you have just learned. Write down your observation. In time, this small daily habit will build your ability to connect macroeconomics to market movements effortlessly.

title: Article 1.2 - The Bank of Tanzania and the Price of Money
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series

In the previous article, we learned that the economy is the sum of all transactions, and that its growth or contraction shapes the environment in which every company operates. But who controls the temperature of that environment? Who decides whether money flows freely or becomes expensive and scarce?

The answer is the Bank of Tanzania (BOT) — our central bank. Understanding what the BOT does, why it makes the decisions it makes, and how those decisions reach your investment portfolio is one of the most powerful advantages an investor can have. This article explains it in plain language, with practical examples from the Dar es Salaam Stock Exchange.
1. What Is a Central Bank, and What Does It Want?
Every country has a central bank. In Tanzania, it’s the Bank of Tanzania, established by the Bank of Tanzania Act. Think of the BOT as the guardian of the financial system. Its primary job is to:
Keep inflation under control — so the money in your pocket keeps its value.
Maintain a stable currency — so businesses and investors can plan.
Supervise commercial banks — so your deposits are safe.
Manage the country’s foreign reserves — so we can pay for imports and service external debt.
For investors, the single most important function is monetary policy — the art of controlling how much money circulates in the economy and at what price.
2. The Price of Money: The Benchmark Interest Rate

Money, just like maize or fuel, has a price. The price of money is the interest rate. When you borrow from a bank, you pay interest. When you deposit savings, you earn interest. The BOT sets a benchmark rate (called the Central Bank Rate, or CBR) that influences all other interest rates in the economy.
When the BOT lowers the CBR:
Commercial banks can borrow from the BOT more cheaply.
They, in turn, lower the rates they charge businesses and individuals.
Borrowing increases. Companies expand, consumers spend, and economic activity picks up.
On the DSE, this is generally positive for stocks — especially banks, whose loan books grow, and consumer companies, whose customers now have more to spend.
When the BOT raises the CBR:
Borrowing becomes expensive.
Businesses delay expansion, consumers cut spending.
Stock prices often fall because future profits are now discounted at a higher rate, and economic growth slows.
So, the BOT’s interest rate decision ripples directly into the share prices you track daily.

Tanzania in focus: In recent years, the BOT has kept the CBR relatively stable, often in the range of 5–6%. This stability has encouraged long-term capital allocation into equities and bonds. In our Daily DSE Wrap on 29 June 2026, we noted that the banking index (BI) reached 19,528.67 — a level that reflects, in part, a stable rate environment that allows banks to lend profitably.
3. How the BOT Controls Money Supply
The BOT does not just set an interest rate and walk away. It actively manages liquidity through several tools:

Treasury bill and bond auctions: The BOT issues government securities on behalf of the Treasury. When it wants to reduce money in circulation (to fight inflation), it sells more securities, pulling cash out of the banking system. When it wants to inject cash, it buys back securities or reduces the amount it sells.
Reserve requirements: Commercial banks must keep a portion of their deposits at the BOT. Raising this requirement means banks have less to lend; lowering it frees up funds.
Open market operations: The BOT buys or sells securities in the secondary market to fine-tune liquidity day-to-day.

For a DSE investor, the most visible of these is the bond market. When the 29 June 2026 DSE report showed TZS 16.20 billion in bond trades, much of that activity was driven by institutional investors reacting to the BOT’s monetary stance. A heavy bond auction can drain cash that might otherwise have found its way into equities — a dynamic we often discuss in the Daily Wrap under “Rotation Signals.”
4. How Interest Rates Affect Different DSE Sectors
Not all sectors react the same way to a BOT rate decision. Here’s a practical guide:
So, when the BOT speaks, you should immediately think: Which sectors in my portfolio benefit, and which could be hurt?
5. Inflation: Why the BOT Will Never Let It Run Wild

Inflation is the rate at which prices rise. A moderate amount of inflation (around 3–5%) is normal and even healthy — it means the economy is growing. But high inflation destroys wealth. Imagine you own a stock that returns 8% per year, but inflation is 10%. In real terms, you are losing money.

The BOT’s single most important job is to prevent high inflation. It does this by raising interest rates when the economy overheats. For equity investors, the transition from a low-inflation, low-rate environment to a high-inflation, high-rate environment can be painful — stock valuations compress, and bond yields become more attractive relative to dividends.

We track inflation closely in the Daily DSE Wrap. Whenever the National Bureau of Statistics releases the monthly CPI figure, or whenever the BOT issues a monetary policy statement, we analyse the implications for the market. Those updates are your direct bridge between the macro concepts in this article and the practical investment decisions you face.
6. How to Track the BOT’s Actions Yourself
You don’t need inside access. Use these free public sources:
BOT website (): Monetary policy statements, auction results, and the monthly economic review. Bookmark it.
DSE daily reports: Already on your site. The bond turnover data tells you if institutions are chasing yield or parking cash.
News media: Major BOT decisions are covered by The Citizen, Daily News, and Bloomberg Africa.
Our Daily DSE Wrap: We explicitly connect BOT policy shifts to the day’s equity and bond market movements.
7. From Macro to Market: The Daily DSE Wrap Connection

In every Daily DSE Wrap, the Market Pulse section gives you the day’s bond activity, foreign flow, and index movements. When you see a spike in bond turnover or a sudden rally in banking stocks, the root cause is often a BOT action or a shift in rate expectations. By understanding how the central bank operates, you can anticipate these moves rather than just observe them.
Next time you read the Daily Wrap, ask yourself: What did the BOT do recently that might explain this sector’s performance?
If you want to learn how to apply this to individual banking stocks, proceed to our article (Module 4.3).
Key Takeaway

The Bank of Tanzania controls the price of money. When money is cheap, businesses grow, consumers spend, and stock markets tend to rise. When money becomes expensive, the opposite happens. As an investor, you cannot control these cycles, but you can understand them — and that understanding gives you an enormous edge over those who simply guess.
Put It Into Practice

Open today’s Daily DSE Wrap and identify the bond turnover figure. Now ask yourself: What does this tell me about where the big money is flowing — into fixed income or equities? The answer is often the key to the next market move.

title: Article 1.3 - Inflation: The Silent Erosion of Your Wealth
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series

In the previous articles, we learned how the economy grows and how the Bank of Tanzania controls the price of money. Now we examine a force that can undo all your hard work without a single headline: inflation. It doesn’t crash markets; it slowly, quietly eats away the purchasing power of your savings and your investment returns. For a Tanzanian investor, understanding inflation is not optional — it’s the foundation of every real return calculation.
1. What Is Inflation?

Inflation is the rate at which the general level of prices for goods and services rises over time. When inflation is 5% per year, a basket of goods that cost TZS 10,000 last year costs TZS 10,500 this year. Your money has lost purchasing power.

In Tanzania, the National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) measures inflation using the Consumer Price Index (CPI), which tracks a fixed basket of commonly purchased items — food, housing, transport, clothing, and so on. The headline inflation figure you hear in the news is the year-on-year change in that CPI.
2. Why Inflation Matters to an Investor

Imagine you keep TZS 1,000,000 in a savings account earning 2% interest. In a year, you have TZS 1,020,000. But if inflation ran at 7% over the same period, the things you could buy for TZS 1,000,000 a year ago now cost TZS 1,070,000. Your “gain” is actually a loss of TZS 50,000 in real terms.

The same logic applies to stocks and bonds. If a company’s earnings grow by 8% but inflation is 6%, the real growth is only about 2%. If a government bond pays a 14% coupon but inflation spikes to 15%, your real return is negative.
The goal of every investor is not just to make money, but to earn a return that beats inflation after taxes. This is the “real return” mindset.
3. How Inflation Moves Through the Economy and Hits Different DSE Sectors
Inflation does not affect all companies equally. Some can pass rising costs to customers; others cannot. Here’s how it typically plays out on the DSE:

In the Daily DSE Wrap, we note inflationary pressures when they appear — for example, if the BOT signals concern about rising food prices, we would flag that consumer-facing stocks could face margin pressure.
4. The BOT’s Role: Fighting Inflation with Interest Rates

As we covered in , the Bank of Tanzania’s primary weapon against inflation is the Central Bank Rate (CBR). When inflation rises above the BOT’s target (typically around 5%), it will raise the CBR. Higher rates cool the economy by making borrowing more expensive, which reduces spending and eventually slows price rises.

For an equity investor, this creates a difficult trade-off: higher rates protect the purchasing power of the shilling, but they also increase the discount rate applied to future corporate earnings, which can push stock prices lower in the short term. The key is to distinguish between temporary inflation (caused by a drought or a fuel price shock) and entrenched inflation (caused by excessive money printing or a wage-price spiral). Temporary inflation often reverses without drastic rate hikes; entrenched inflation forces the BOT to act decisively.
5. Inflation in Tanzania: What the Data Tells Us

Tanzania has generally enjoyed moderate inflation in the 3–6% range over the past several years, a stark contrast to the double-digit inflation of the early 2010s. This stability is one reason the DSE All Share Index (DSEI) has trended higher: investors are not panicked by runaway prices, and companies can plan with reasonable confidence.
However, investors must always watch for shocks:
Food prices: Tanzania is an agricultural economy. A drought can send maize prices soaring, which feeds directly into the CPI and reduces rural incomes.
Fuel prices: Oil is imported, so global crude prices directly affect transport and manufacturing costs.
Exchange rate pass-through: A weakening shilling makes imports more expensive, which can stoke inflation.
We monitor these channels in our Daily DSE Wrap. When the DSEI moves sharply, the explanation is often found in one of these inflationary or deflationary pressures.
6. How to Inflation-Proof Your Portfolio
No portfolio can be completely immune to inflation, but you can take practical steps:
Favour companies with pricing power — those that sell essential products and can raise prices without losing customers. Breweries, mobile networks, and dominant banks often fit this category.
Include dividend-paying stocks — companies that can maintain or grow dividends during inflationary periods provide a cash return that helps offset rising living costs.
Consider inflation-linked bonds — the Tanzanian government occasionally issues bonds whose coupon or principal adjusts with inflation. These offer direct protection.
Stay diversified — equities, bonds, real estate, and cash each respond differently to inflation. A well-built portfolio balances these exposures.
Reinvest dividends and interest — compounding at a rate that beats inflation is the most reliable way to build real wealth over time.
7. From Macro to Market: The Daily DSE Wrap Connection

In every Daily DSE Wrap, we report the day’s market snapshot, but we also pay attention to the macro context. When we note that bond turnover is surging while equities are flat, it might signal that large investors are moving money into fixed income because they expect higher inflation and therefore higher interest rates. When we highlight a rally in banking stocks, it might reflect a stable inflation outlook that supports steady loan growth.
Next time you read the Daily Wrap, ask: What is the inflation story today, and how might it explain the sector moves I’m seeing?
If you want to dive deeper into how to evaluate a specific bank’s ability to handle inflation, proceed to our article (Module 4.3).
Key Takeaway

Inflation is the silent thief of wealth. It does not announce itself with a market crash, but it steadily erodes the value of every shilling you hold. The only defence is to invest in assets that can grow faster than prices rise. Understanding inflation — and how the BOT fights it — equips you to make decisions that preserve and grow your real purchasing power over the long term.
Put It Into Practice

Check today’s Daily DSE Wrap and find the bond turnover figure. Is it high or low relative to recent days? Then ask yourself: What does this tell me about where large investors think inflation and interest rates are heading?

title: Article 2.1 - Supply, Demand, and the Business That Sells It
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series

In Module 1, we explored the broad economy. Now we zoom in to the individual company. The most fundamental tool for understanding a business is the law of supply and demand — the same force that determines the price of onions in Kariakoo also determines the profits of TBL, VODA, and CRDB.
The Basics
Supply is how much producers are willing to sell at a given price. Higher prices usually mean more supply.
Demand is how much consumers are willing to buy. Lower prices boost demand.
Equilibrium is where supply meets demand — the market price.
For a business, the goal is to sell at a price well above the cost of production. The difference is profit.

Pricing Power Not all companies can raise prices without losing customers. A company with pricing power can increase prices steadily over time without significantly reducing demand. This power comes from:
Strong brand (TBL’s Safari Lager commands loyalty).
Few competitors (Tanzania’s cement industry has limited players, aiding TCC and TPCC).
High switching costs (a bank where a client holds a mortgage, salary account, and mobile banking is hard to leave).
Companies with pricing power are generally better investments because they protect margins during inflation and can reinvest profits for growth.

Demand Shifts Demand changes due to rising incomes, population growth, or new technology. As Tanzania’s middle class expands, demand for financial services, mobile data, and branded goods increases, benefiting CRDB, NMB, and VODA. When the government invests in infrastructure, demand for cement rises, boosting TCC and TPCC. In the Daily DSE Wrap, turnover and volume reveal where demand is flowing in real time.

Supply-Side Risks Even strong demand won’t help a company that can’t deliver. Drought, power outages, or import delays can crush margins. For example, TOL, which imports fuel, faces supply risks if global oil prices spike or the shilling weakens. Ask: Where do raw materials come from? How dependent is the company on imports? Does it have reliable power and water?

Daily DSE Wrap Connection Every volume spike and price movement is supply and demand at work. On 29 June 2026, NMB traded 228,782 shares (including a block trade) — that’s demand meeting supply at a mutually agreeable price near TZS 16,160. Sellers were not desperate and buyers were willing to pay.

title: Article 2.2 - How to Read a Company’s Financial Statements (Part 1 – The Income Statement)
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
Financial statements are the report card of a business. The first and most watched is the income statement (profit and loss statement). It answers: Is this company making money?
Anatomy of an Income Statement Every income statement follows this structure:
Revenue (Sales): Total money from selling goods or services.
– Cost of Goods Sold (COGS): Direct costs of production.
= Gross Profit
– Operating Expenses: Salaries, rent, marketing, depreciation.
= Operating Profit (EBIT)
– Interest Expense + Interest Income – Tax
= Net Profit
For a Tanzanian bank like CRDB, interest income from loans is revenue; interest paid on deposits is COGS; the difference is net interest income. After branch and staff costs, we get operating profit.
Margins: The True Test
Gross Margin = (Gross Profit / Revenue) × 100.
Operating Margin = (Operating Profit / Revenue) × 100.
Net Margin = (Net Profit / Revenue) × 100.
Compare margins over time and across competitors. A declining margin may signal rising costs, competition, or poor management. A stable or expanding margin is positive.

Real DSE Application When you read an annual report, ask: Is revenue growing? Are costs growing faster than revenue? Is net profit sustainable or boosted by one-off items? The Daily DSE Wrap often notes earnings results — for example, if NMB’s net interest margin improved due to lower funding costs, that’s the income statement at work.

title: Article 2.3 - How to Read a Company’s Financial Statements (Part 2 – The Balance Sheet)
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
The balance sheet shows what a company owns and owes at a single point in time. It reveals hidden strengths or looming dangers the income statement alone misses.
The Fundamental Equation Assets = Liabilities + Shareholders’ Equity
Assets are what the company owns: cash, buildings, inventory, receivables.
Liabilities are what it owes: loans, supplier payables, bonds.
Shareholders’ Equity is the net worth left for owners after debts are paid.
Key Items
Cash and equivalents: Can the company survive a shock?
Accounts receivable: Money owed by customers. Fast-growing receivables may signal collection problems.
Inventory: Too much ties up cash; too little may lose sales.
Property, plant & equipment (PPE): Fixed assets. Heavy investment signals expansion but also depreciation.
Short-term debt: Must be manageable relative to cash and cash flow.
Long-term debt: Excessive debt can choke a company in a downturn.
Retained earnings: Cumulative profits not paid as dividends; show how much has been reinvested.

The Debt-to-Equity Ratio Total Liabilities / Shareholders’ Equity. A ratio above 2.0 is high for non-financial companies. Banks naturally have higher leverage. Check this ratio against peers and over time. For example, TCC and TPCC, as cement manufacturers, may carry significant debt for plant construction, but their debt should be serviceable from operating cash flow.

Working Capital Current Assets – Current Liabilities. Positive working capital means the company can meet short-term obligations. A worsening trend may signal collection or inventory problems. Watch for a sudden spike in receivables without a corresponding rise in revenue — that could mean customers are delaying payments, tying up cash.

Daily DSE Wrap Connection When we say “CRDB has a strong capital base,” that’s a balance sheet statement. The health of the balance sheet determines whether a company can pay dividends, invest, or survive a recession.

title: Article 2.4 - The Cash Flow Statement: Where the Money Really Is
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
Profit is an opinion; cash is a fact. A company can report healthy net profit yet go bankrupt if it runs out of cash. The cash flow statement shows actual money movement.
Three Sections
Operating Activities: Cash from core business — sales, salaries, collections. Positive operating cash flow is essential.
Investing Activities: Cash spent on assets (new factories) or received from selling them. Negative investing cash flow may be okay if expansion is productive and funded by operations.
Financing Activities: Cash from issuing shares or loans, and cash paid as dividends or debt repayments.

Free Cash Flow (FCF) FCF = Operating Cash Flow – Capital Expenditures. FCF is the cash left after maintaining or expanding the asset base. It can pay dividends, reduce debt, or fund growth. Companies with strong, growing FCF are prized.

Real DSE Application A cement company might have strong operating cash flow but heavy investing cash flow if building a new plant. That’s fine if the expansion will generate future returns. But if operating cash flow is weak and it’s borrowing heavily, that’s a red flag. In the Daily DSE Wrap, dividend announcements reflect cash flow strength — like TBL’s TZS 818 per share dividend signalling ample FCF.
Further Reading: Understand what protects a company’s profits in .

APPLY IT NOW For any DSE stock you hold, look up its latest cash flow statement. Calculate free cash flow. Is it positive? Has it grown over the last three years? Compare it to net profit. If FCF is far below net profit, investigate why.

title: Article 2.5 - Competitive Moats: Why Some Companies Stay on Top
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
Why can a brewery raise prices without losing customers? Why does one bank dominate lending? The answer is a competitive moat — a durable advantage that protects a company from rivals.
Types of Moats
Brand: Consumers trust and pay a premium. (TBL’s Safari Lager)
Cost advantage: Producing at lower cost through scale or superior technology. (TPCC’s local limestone reserves)
Network effect: Service becomes more valuable as more people use it. (VODA’s mobile money)
Regulatory licence: Limited competition through licensing. (Banking licences from BOT)
High switching costs: Customers find it expensive or inconvenient to leave. (Corporate clients with multiple accounts at CRDB)

Identifying a Moat Ask: Can the company raise prices without losing significant sales? Does it consistently earn higher returns on equity (ROE) than peers? Has its market share been stable or growing? Would a new competitor struggle to replicate what it does? If yes, a moat likely exists.

Moats and the DSE In the Daily Wrap, we note when a company’s loan growth outpaces peers (branch network moat) or when TBL’s volumes hold up despite a price increase (brand loyalty). Moats are not permanent — technology, regulation, or poor management can erode them.

Action Step: Pick your favourite DSE stock. Write down its competitive moat. Test that belief: could a competitor with enough money replicate it?

title: Article 2.6 - How Management Decisions Create (or Destroy) Value
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
Even a great company with a wide moat can be ruined by poor management. Conversely, a mediocre business can be transformed by an exceptional leadership team.
Five Key Management Decisions
Capital allocation: Invests in high-return projects; returns excess cash to shareholders. Warning: chasing growth for its own sake, expensive acquisitions.
Capital structure: Maintains prudent debt; matches funding to asset life. Warning: excessive short-term debt.
Dividend policy: Sustainable, growing dividends, retaining enough for reinvestment. Warning: sudden cuts without explanation; borrowing to pay dividends.
Transparency: Open about strategy, risks, performance. Warning: complex language, frequent accounting changes.
Culture & Governance: Ethical, long-oriented; independent board. Warning: conflicts of interest, family members in key roles without merit.
Assessing Management on the DSE
Read the chairman’s and CEO’s letters. Are they honest about challenges?
Compare ROE over time. Consistent ROE suggests disciplined capital allocation.
Check insider trading announcements. Heavy director selling without reason is a red flag.
Review dividend history. Stable or growing dividends signal confidence.
In the Daily Wrap, we sometimes note management announcements — a major investment, a CEO change — and analyse through this lens.

title: Article 3.1 - What Is a Stock Exchange? Inside the Dar es Salaam Stock Exchange
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
We now shift to the arena where companies and investors meet. The Dar es Salaam Stock Exchange (DSE) is the marketplace where ownership of Tanzanian companies is bought and sold.
Purpose of a Stock Exchange
Capital raising: Companies sell shares to the public (IPO, rights issue) for expansion.
Liquidity: Investors can buy and sell shares easily.
Price discovery: Through thousands of buyers and sellers, the market determines fair prices.
The DSE operates under CMSA regulation.

The DSE: Brief History and Structure Incorporated in 1996, trading began in 1998. It now lists over 20 companies, including cross-listings from Kenya (KCB, NMG). Trading days are Monday to Friday, 10:00 am to 3:00 pm EAT. The automated trading system matches orders; settlement is through the Central Securities Depository (CSD), which holds shares electronically.
DSE Indices
DSE All Share Index (DSEI): Tracks all listed companies.
Tanzania Share Index (TSI): Excludes cross-listed companies.
Sector indices: BI (banks), IA (industrials), CS (commercial services).
Every Daily DSE Wrap reports these indices so you can see which segments are moving.
Market Participants
Retail investors
Institutional investors (pension funds, insurance, UTT AMIS)
Foreign investors
Brokers and market makers (developing)
The Wrap’s foreign flow data tells you what percentage of turnover came from foreign buyers and sellers — a critical barometer of international sentiment.

title: Article 3.2 - Stocks, Bonds, and ETFs: The Three Building Blocks
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
On the DSE, you buy specific securities, each with its own risk, return, and purpose.

Stocks (Equities) Buying a share of CRDB or TBL makes you a part-owner. You share in profits (dividends, capital gains) and risks. Stocks offer the highest long-term return potential but are the most volatile.

Bonds A bond is a loan to the government or a company in exchange for fixed interest (coupon) and return of principal at maturity. Bonds are generally safer, offering lower returns. They suit conservative investors or as a portfolio stabiliser. The DSE bond market is active; on 29 June 2026, TZS 16.20 billion in bonds traded, with a 25-year bond yielding 15.95% heavily traded.

ETFs (Exchange-Traded Funds) An ETF holds a basket of stocks or bonds and trades like a single stock. The DSE lists IEACLC-ETF and VERTEX-ETF. ETFs provide instant diversification. On 29 June, IEACLC-ETF traded 354,823 shares at TZS 1,250.
Portfolio Building A balanced investor might hold 60% equities, 30% bonds, 10% cash. The exact mix depends on age, goals, and risk tolerance.

title: Article 3.3 - How a Trade Happens: From Your Phone to the Market
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
You decide to buy 100 shares of CRDB. What happens next?
Placing the Order Contact a licensed DSE stockbroker and specify:
Ticker (e.g., CRDB)
Quantity (number of shares)
Price limit (maximum you will pay, or minimum you will accept if selling) Your broker enters the order into the DSE’s Automated Trading System (ATS).

The Order Book The ATS maintains an electronic order book — lists of all outstanding buy (bid) and sell (ask/offer) orders, sorted by price and time. The best bid is the highest price any buyer will pay; the best ask is the lowest any seller will accept. The difference is the spread. A trade occurs when a buy order matches a sell order.

In the DSE daily price tables, you see “Outstanding Bids” and “Outstanding Offers” — the depth of demand and supply at the close. For a stock like CRDB, a deep bid side (e.g., 225,734 bids on 29 June versus 82,285 offers) tells you buyers are more eager than sellers, which can push the price up. For a less liquid stock, a thin bid side means selling even a modest number of shares could push the price down significantly.

Clearing and Settlement Once executed, the DSE sends details to the Central Securities Depository (CSD). Ownership records update: shares move from seller’s account to yours, cash moves between brokers’ settlement accounts. This takes T+2 (trade date plus two business days). Your shares are held electronically; you receive a contract note.

Why This Matters Understanding mechanics helps you set realistic limit orders. If the spread is wide (e.g., bid at 2,550, ask at 2,650), a limit order at the midpoint may not fill quickly. High volume and many deals signal strong liquidity; low volume with wide spreads suggests caution.

Action Step: Look at the DSE daily price table on our website. Pick a stock and note the spread. Is it narrow (liquid) or wide (illiquid)?

title: Article 3.4 - Understanding Market Data: Turnover, Volume, and Deals
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
Every trading day, the DSE publishes numbers like “Turnover: TZS 7,088.98 million, Volume: 1,734,477 shares, Deals: 4,704.” They are the market’s voice.

Turnover: The Money Flow Total value of shares traded. A rising turnover trend suggests growing interest and confidence. A sudden spike without a price move may indicate a large institutional block trade. On 29 June 2026, turnover was TZS 7.09 billion — down from a record but still well above the quiet-day level of TZS 3 billion, signalling a liquid, active market.

Volume: The Quantity of Shares Traded High volume on CRDB (928,067 shares) indicates deep liquidity — you can trade a large block without dramatically moving the price. A rising price on expanding volume confirms genuine demand. A rising price on declining volume may be fragile.

Deals: The Participation Count Separate buy-sell transactions. Many deals with moderate volume = broad retail participation. Few deals with high volume = large block trades. On 29 June, 4,704 deals with moderate volume indicated healthy, domestically driven activity.
Putting It Together Strong, broad-based buying: turnover surges, volume jumps, deals spike, DSEI rises. A single large cross-trade: turnover rises but volume and deals fall, DSEI barely moves.

title: Article 3.5 - The Difference Between Primary and Secondary Markets
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series

When you buy CRDB shares through your broker on a Tuesday, you’re in the secondary market — trading already-issued securities. But CRDB originally sold shares to the public in an IPO in the primary market.
Primary Market: Companies Raise Fresh Capital The issuer sells new securities directly to investors. Proceeds go to the company. Examples:
IPO (Initial Public Offering): A company lists on the DSE for the first time.
Rights issue: Existing shareholders buy additional shares at a discount.
Bond auction: Government or corporation issues new bonds.
Primary offerings affect existing shareholders (dilution in a rights issue) and signal growth plans. The Daily Wrap covers these when announced.

Secondary Market: Investors Trade Among Themselves This is the DSE’s daily trading — buying and selling between investors. No new capital flows to the company. It provides liquidity and fair price discovery. The DSEI reflects secondary market prices.
Why the Distinction Matters
IPO shares are often priced at a discount; secondary trading determines the real price.
A rights issue dilutes your ownership unless you participate.
Buying a bond at auction and holding to maturity avoids price fluctuations; buying in the secondary market exposes you to interest rate risk.

title: Article 4.1 - What Is Fundamental Analysis? The Art of Finding True Value
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series

You now understand the economy, companies, and the marketplace. Fundamental analysis is the process of evaluating a company’s intrinsic value by examining its financial statements, competitive position, industry, and the broader economy. If intrinsic value is significantly above the market price, the stock may be undervalued — a buying opportunity.

Price vs. Value Benjamin Graham taught that in the short run the market is a voting machine (popularity), but in the long run it is a weighing machine (substance). Your job is to estimate substance — the true worth — and compare it to the current market price. Intrinsic value is a range, based on reasonable assumptions about future cash flows, growth, and risk.
Two Main Approaches
Relative valuation: Compare the company’s price to earnings, book value, or sales, then compare those multiples to peers or history (covered in 4.2).
Discounted cash flow (DCF): Project future free cash flows and discount them back to today’s shillings. More complex but forces deep thinking.
For most DSE investors, relative valuation using simple multiples is a practical start.
The Fundamental Analysis Checklist
Understand the business.
Assess the industry.
Examine financials (revenue, margins, debt, cash flow).
Evaluate management.
Calculate valuation.
Consider macro risks (interest rates, exchange rates, regulation).

title: Article 4.2 - Key Valuation Ratios Made Simple (P/E, P/B, Dividend Yield)
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
Ratios are the shorthand of fundamental analysis. They let you compare companies of different sizes and assess whether a stock is cheap or expensive at a glance.

Price-to-Earnings Ratio (P/E) P/E = Share Price / Earnings per Share (EPS). It tells you how many years of current earnings it would take to recoup the purchase price. High P/E suggests expected strong future growth; low P/E may signal a bargain — or a company in trouble. On the DSE, banks often trade at P/E ratios between 5 and 12. If CRDB earns TZS 250 per share and trades at TZS 2,590, its P/E is about 10.4 — reasonable for a stable bank in a growing economy.

Price-to-Book Ratio (P/B) P/B = Share Price / Book Value per Share. Book value is assets minus liabilities — net worth per share. P/B below 1.0 means the market values the company at less than its accounting net worth. Banks are often analysed using P/B. If NMB trades at TZS 16,160 and has a book value per share of, say, TZS 10,000, its P/B is 1.62. That premium suggests the market expects NMB to generate returns well above its book value.

Dividend Yield Dividend Yield = Annual Dividend per Share / Share Price × 100%. This tells you the cash return just for holding the stock. On 29 June, TBL traded at TZS 9,710 and paid an annual dividend of TZS 818, a yield of 8.4%. High yield can be a trap if the dividend is unsustainable.

Using Ratios Wisely Compare a company’s ratios to its own history, industry peers, and the overall market. The Daily Wrap occasionally references valuation context — like “the banking sector trades at a discount to regional peers on a price-to-book basis.”

title: Article 4.3 - How to Analyse a Sector: Banking
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
Banks dominate the DSE. CRDB, NMB, KCB, DCB, and others are among the most traded. Analysing a bank is different because its raw material is money.

The Banking Business Model Banks take deposits (pay interest) and lend (charge higher interest). The difference is the net interest margin (NIM). They also earn fee income from transactions, forex, and mobile banking.
Key drivers: loan book growth, fee income, cost control. Key risks: credit risk (defaults), interest rate risk, liquidity risk.
Metrics That Matter
NIM: 5–8% is typical in Tanzania.
Return on Equity (ROE): Above 15% is good.
Return on Assets (ROA): Above 1.5% is good.
Cost-to-Income Ratio: Below 50% is efficient.
NPL Ratio: Below 5% is comfortable.
Loan-to-Deposit Ratio: 70–90% balances profitability and liquidity.
Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR): Above the regulatory minimum (12%+).
Step-by-Step Bank Analysis
Read the annual report.
Calculate NIM, ROE, ROA, cost-to-income for three years.
Examine NPL ratio. Which sectors are struggling?
Check CAR.
Assess competitive position.
Consider macro environment.
Further Reading: Next, analyse industrials and consumer goods in .
Action Step: Pick one DSE bank. Download its latest annual report. Calculate NIM, ROE, and NPL ratio. Compare to another DSE bank. Which is stronger?

title: Article 4.4 - How to Analyse a Sector: Industrials & Consumer Goods
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
Tanzania’s industrial and consumer goods sector includes TBL, TCC, TPCC, TOL, and TCCL. These companies make things — beer, cement, oil products — and their fortunes are tied to the physical economy.
The Industrial Business Model Industrial companies transform raw materials into finished products. Key drivers:
Capacity utilisation: How much of the factory’s potential output is produced.
Input costs: Raw materials, energy, transport.
Pricing power: Ability to pass cost increases to customers.
Demand: Driven by population growth, urbanisation, and economic expansion.
Key Metrics
Revenue growth (volume vs. price).
Gross margin (pricing power and cost control).
Operating margin (factory and distribution efficiency).
Return on capital employed (ROCE): Higher is better.
Debt-to-equity: Ensure debt is serviceable.
Capacity expansion plans: Future growth with execution risk.
Consumer Goods Specifics Brewers, cigarette makers, food processors depend on brand loyalty, distribution, and volume. Also watch:
Market share.
Product mix (premium vs. value).
Excise tax sensitivity (a budget change can significantly impact earnings).
Further Reading: Learn to extract the full story from an annual report in .
Action Step: Compare the gross margins of TBL and TCC over three years. Which has more consistent pricing power? Why?

title: Article 4.5 - Reading the Story Behind the Numbers: Annual Report Analysis
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
The annual report is the single most important document a public company produces. It contains the financial statements, management’s narrative, the auditor’s opinion, and detailed notes.
Where to Start
Chairman’s / CEO’s letter: Sets the tone. Does management acknowledge challenges? Look for specific goals and whether previous goals were met.
Auditor’s report: Look for “unqualified opinion.” Any “qualified” opinion is a red flag.
Financial statements: Income statement, balance sheet, cash flow statement.
Notes to the financial statements: Often the most revealing part. They explain accounting policies, breakdown debt, detail contingent liabilities, and disclose related-party transactions.
Red Flags
Frequent changes in accounting policies.
Large related-party transactions not at arm’s length.
Rising receivables outpacing revenue growth.
Overly complex language.
Dividend cut without explanation.

title: Article 4.6 - An Introduction to Technical Analysis (It’s Not Sorcery)
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
While fundamental analysis tells you what to buy, technical analysis attempts to tell you when by studying price charts, volume, and patterns. It’s a supplementary tool, not a crystal ball.

The Foundation: Price and Trends A trend is the general direction of prices. An uptrend has higher highs and higher lows; a downtrend has lower lows and lower highs. The simplest rule: Don’t fight the trend. A rising price with rising volume suggests accumulation; a falling price with high volume suggests distribution.
Support and Resistance
Support: A price level where buying interest prevents further decline (a floor).
Resistance: A price level where selling pressure halts a rally (a ceiling).
If CRDB consistently finds buyers at TZS 2,550, that’s support; if it fails to break above TZS 2,600, that’s resistance.
Volume as Confirmation A breakout above resistance on significantly higher volume is more likely genuine. A breakout on low volume may be false.

Simple Moving Averages (SMA) A 50-day SMA is the average closing price over the last 50 trading days, smoothing noise. Price above the 50-day SMA suggests a positive medium-term trend; below, negative.

Limitations on the DSE Technical analysis works best in liquid, continuous markets. The DSE’s lower liquidity on some counters means a few large orders can create misleading patterns. Use it as a supplement to, never a replacement for, fundamental analysis.

title: Article 5.1 - Risk and Return: The Only Free Lunch Is Diversification
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
Every investment carries risk. The key is not to avoid it — impossible — but to understand, manage, and be compensated for bearing it.
Types of Investment Risk
Company-specific risk: Can be diversified away by holding many companies.
Sector risk: Partially diversifiable by holding different sectors.
Market risk (systematic): Affects all assets. Managed through asset allocation and a long time horizon.
The Risk-Return Trade-Off Higher expected return demands higher risk. Government bonds are safe with lower returns; equities offer higher returns but can lose significant value.

Diversification: The Free Lunch Spreading money across different companies, sectors, and asset classes reduces risk without necessarily reducing expected return. A simple diversified DSE portfolio might hold a major bank (CRDB/NMB), a consumer goods company (TBL), an industrial (TCC/TPCC), a telecom (VODA), and a bond or bond fund.

title: Article 5.2 - Building Your First Investment Portfolio
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
Now it’s time to build. A robust portfolio starts with your goal and time horizon.
Define Your Goal and Time Horizon
Saving for retirement in 20 years? More equity risk.
Saving for a house in 5 years? Lean toward bonds and stable dividend stocks.
Generating monthly income? Focus on high-dividend stocks and bonds.
Sample Allocation Framework
Within equities, spread across at least three sectors. Example moderate portfolio: CRDB 20%, TBL 15%, VODA 10%, TPCC 10%, UTT AMIS Bond Fund 35%, cash 10%.
Starting Small You don’t need millions. TZS 500,000 can buy a few shares and a bond fund. The most important step is to start. Time in the market beats timing the market.
Further Reading: Discover the magic of compounding in .
Action Step: Write down your target allocation. Compare to actual holdings. Plan to close the biggest gap over three months.

title: Article 5.3 - The Power of Compounding and Why Time Beats Timing
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
Einstein reportedly called compound interest the eighth wonder of the world. For a Tanzanian investor, compounding is the single most powerful wealth-building force.

What Is Compounding? Earning returns on your returns. Invest TZS 1,000,000 at 10%: after one year, TZS 1,100,000. Next year, you earn 10% on TZS 1,100,000 (TZS 110,000), not on the original TZS 1,000,000. Over years, this snowballs.

The Mathematics TZS 1,000,000 at 10% annual return: 5 years → TZS 1,610,510; 10 years → TZS 2,593,742; 20 years → TZS 6,727,500; 30 years → TZS 17,449,402. Notice the last 10 years added over TZS 10 million with no new money. Reinvesting dividends accelerates this further.

Time in the Market vs. Timing the Market Trying to buy at the bottom and sell at the top rarely works consistently. Missing just the best 10 trading days in a decade can halve your returns. The fully invested, dividend-reinvesting, noise-ignoring investor almost always outperforms the trader.
Further Reading: Understand your own psychology in .

APPLY IT NOW Use an online compound interest calculator. Input your current monthly investment amount, an assumed 9% annual return, and a 20-year horizon. How much would you have? Now halve the return to 6% — how much difference? Finally, increase the monthly contribution by just TZS 20,000. The result will prove that consistency beats both luck and genius.

title: Article 5.4 - Behavioural Finance: Understanding Your Own Worst Enemy
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
You can master all analysis techniques and still fail if you cannot master your own emotions. Behavioural finance studies psychological biases that cause irrational decisions.

Loss Aversion Pain of losing is about twice as powerful as pleasure of gaining. This leads to holding onto losers too long and selling winners too early. Fix: Have a predetermined thesis for every investment. If the thesis is broken, sell regardless. If intact, hold through noise.

Herd Mentality The temptation to buy when everyone is buying and sell when everyone is panicking. Herding creates bubbles and crashes. Fix: Use a checklist (Article 4.7) and a long-term plan. Re-read your written investment policy.

Overconfidence After a few successes, investors believe they have a special talent for prediction. They trade more, take larger positions, and suffer disproportionate losses. Fix: Track your decisions. Compare your actual returns to the DSEI. If not beating the index over a cycle, simplify and diversify.
Confirmation Bias Noticing only information that supports your existing view, ignoring contradictory evidence. Fix: Actively seek the bear case for every stock you own.
Further Reading: Embrace the Amana Capital philosophy in .
Action Step: Reflect on your last investment decision. Which bias might have been at play? Write it down.

title: Article 5.5 - Long-Horizon Thinking: The Amana Capital Philosophy
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
At Amana Capital East Africa, our investment philosophy rests on four pillars: Integrity without exception, Long-horizon thinking, Deep regional intelligence, and Disciplined conviction.

Integrity Without Exception Trust is the foundation of finance. We are transparent, clearly separate education from advice, and never promise returns we cannot deliver. For you: invest only in what you understand. If a product sounds too good to be true, walk away.

Long-Horizon Thinking The best investment decisions are made with a multi-year perspective. Short-term price movements are noise; long-term value creation matters. Our daily analysis always includes a “Considerations for a Multi-Year Framework” to train your eye beyond daily volatility. For you: set a minimum holding period of, say, three years. Ask: Will this company likely be stronger in three years?

Deep Regional Intelligence We focus on East Africa because that’s what we know best — the local regulatory environment, cultural nuances, and economic drivers. For you: build your portfolio primarily in markets you understand. A Tanzanian investor holding only DSE stocks and bonds is perfectly fine.

Disciplined Conviction Analysis without action is useless. Action without discipline is dangerous. We form a view, act within a structured framework, and hold ourselves accountable. For you: create an Investment Policy Statement — one page stating your goals, allocation, and buying/selling rules. When emotions run high, your policy keeps you grounded.
Further Reading: See how macro trends shape multi-year themes in .

Action Step: Write your own Investment Policy Statement. “I will invest TZS 200,000 per month into a diversified portfolio of DSE equities and a bond fund, and I will not sell for at least five years.” Sign it. Keep it.

title: Article 5.6 - How Macro Trends Shape Multi-Year Investment Themes
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
The big money is made by positioning for trends that unfold over a decade. Several structural shifts are reshaping Tanzania and East Africa.

Urbanisation and the Rise of the Consumer Dar es Salaam, Arusha, Mwanza, and Dodoma are expanding. Urbanisation drives demand for housing, cement, banking, mobile connectivity, and consumer goods. Companies at this intersection — banks, cement manufacturers, telcos, brewers — are likely to see sustained demand for decades.

Regional Integration and Cross-Border Trade The East African Community (EAC) is deepening integration. Cross-listed stocks like KCB and NMG already allow Tanzanians to participate in Kenya’s economy. As trading systems and regulation harmonise, the opportunity set expands. Companies operating across multiple EAC markets will have a growth advantage.

Financial Inclusion and Mobile Money Millions remain unbanked, but mobile money is closing the gap. VODA’s M-Pesa and other platforms bring savings, credit, and insurance to previously excluded populations. This expands the addressable market for financial services and creates new revenue streams for listed companies.

Infrastructure Investment The government’s focus on roads, railways (SGR), ports, and energy will benefit construction, cement, and logistics companies for years. Bond investors can participate directly through government and corporate bonds.

Demographic Dividend Tanzania has one of the world’s youngest populations. A growing working-age population, if productively employed, can drive economic growth for decades. This demographic tailwind supports long-term equity returns.

title: Article 6.1 - How to Open a CSD Account and Buy Your First DSE Stock
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
All the knowledge in the world is useless without action. Here’s exactly how to start.
Open a CSD Account
Select a licensed stockbroker from the DSE’s official list (available on the DSE website or through our contact page).
Visit the broker’s office with:
Copy of your TIN (Taxpayer Identification Number).
Passport-sized photograph.
Copy of national ID, passport, or driver’s licence.
Proof of residence (utility bill or bank statement).
Fill out the CSD account opening forms.
The broker submits your application; you receive an account number.
Fund your brokerage account (minimums can be as low as TZS 100,000).
Place Your First Trade Contact your broker and specify:
Ticker (e.g., CRDB)
Quantity (e.g., 100 shares)
Price limit (e.g., “buy at TZS 2,550 maximum”) The broker executes the order on the DSE ATS. Shares are credited to your CSD account (T+2). You receive a contract note.
Tips for Beginners
Start small with a liquid, well-known company you’ve researched.
Use limit orders, not market orders, to control the price.
Hold for the long term; don’t check prices daily hoping to flip quick profits.

title: Article 6.2 - Investing from Abroad: A Diaspora Step-by-Step Guide
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
Tanzanians living in the UK, Europe, North America, or Asia can invest on the DSE. Distance is not a barrier, but there are additional steps.
Opening a CSD Account from Abroad Many licensed DSE brokers handle remote account opening. You’ll typically need:
Scanned copies of your Tanzanian passport or national ID.
A TIN certificate (apply through the TRA online portal or via a representative).
Proof of overseas address (utility bill, bank statement).
Passport-sized photograph.
Completed account opening forms (provided by the broker).
Contact a broker directly, explain you are in the diaspora, and ask for their remote account opening procedure.

Funding Your Account Transfer funds in hard currency (GBP, USD, EUR) to the broker’s settlement bank account. Funds are converted to TZS at the prevailing exchange rate. Compare exchange rates and fees — specialist services (Wise, WorldRemit, direct SWIFT) may offer better rates than traditional banks. Once shillings are in your brokerage account, you place orders as any local investor would.
Tax Considerations
Withholding tax on dividends: typically 5% for resident individuals. For non-residents, rates may differ under double taxation agreements. Consult a tax advisor.
Capital gains tax: Currently, gains on listed securities are tax-exempt in Tanzania. Your country of residence may tax them. Check local rules.
Estate tax: Ensure your CSD account and holdings are documented in your will or estate plan.

title: Article 6.3 - Understanding Tanzanian Tax on Investments
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
Taxes affect your net return. Tanzania’s tax regime for listed securities is relatively favourable.

Withholding Tax on Dividends Dividends from DSE-listed companies are subject to a 5% final withholding tax for resident individuals. If a company declares TZS 1,000 per share, TZS 950 is deposited, TZS 50 goes to TRA. The tax is final — you do not declare it further. For non-residents, rates vary under double taxation agreements (5–15%). For example, a UK-based investor might pay 15% withholding on dividends, so a TZS 1,000 gross dividend yields TZS 850 net.

Capital Gains Tax on Listed Securities Currently, capital gains from the sale of listed securities are exempt from tax for both residents and non-residents. This is a major advantage. A gain of TZS 1,000,000 is yours entirely. This exemption encourages long-term investment and market participation. Tax laws can change, so stay informed.

Withholding Tax on Bond Interest Interest on government and corporate bonds is subject to a 5% withholding tax for residents. For non-residents, the rate may be higher under certain conditions. For example, a 15.95% government bond coupon would yield approximately 15.15% net for a resident after the 5% withholding tax.

Tax on Collective Investment Schemes If you invest through a unit trust like UTT AMIS, the tax treatment depends on the underlying assets. Generally, fund income distributed to you is subject to withholding tax at the applicable rate.
Record-Keeping Keep all contract notes, dividend vouchers, and annual CSD statements. They are your proof of transactions and will be needed if audited or if you need to reconcile your portfolio.

Action Step: If you hold dividend-paying stocks, check your contract notes to confirm withholding tax has been correctly applied. If uncertain, contact your broker.

title: Article 6.4 - Using Collective Investment Schemes (UTT AMIS, etc.)
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series

Not everyone has the time or expertise to build a diversified portfolio of individual stocks. Collective investment schemes (CIS) — also called unit trusts or mutual funds — pool money from many investors and are managed by professionals. In Tanzania, UTT AMIS is one of the primary providers.
How a Unit Trust Works
You buy units at the prevailing unit price (Net Asset Value per unit, NAV).
The fund invests in a diversified portfolio of stocks, bonds, or both.
Returns come from capital gains on underlying securities and interest/dividend income.
You can buy and redeem units on any dealing day.
Advantages
Instant diversification even with small amounts.
Professional management — the fund manager conducts analysis and trading.
Liquidity — you can usually redeem units relatively quickly.
Regulatory oversight — CIS are regulated by CMSA, providing a layer of protection.
Types of Funds
Equity funds: Invest primarily in stocks. Higher risk, higher potential return.
Bond funds: Invest in government and corporate bonds. Lower risk, stable income.
Balanced funds: Mix of equities and bonds.
Money market funds: Very low risk, highly liquid, often used as a cash management tool.
How to Invest
Visit the fund manager’s office or website.
Complete an application form and provide KYC documents (ID, TIN, photo).
Deposit the minimum investment amount (often TZS 100,000 or less).
Receive a statement showing your unit holdings.
Monitor the fund’s performance and add to your investment regularly.
Further Reading: Form the habit with .

Action Step: Research two CIS providers in Tanzania. Compare their fees, minimum investments, and historical performance. Consider allocating a small portion of your portfolio to a bond or balanced fund as a diversifier.

title: Article 6.5 - Building a Monthly Investment Habit
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
The most successful investors are the most consistent. Building a monthly investment habit — automatically, regardless of market conditions — is the surest path to long-term wealth.

Shilling-Cost Averaging Instead of investing a large lump sum at once, you invest a fixed amount at regular intervals (e.g., TZS 200,000 every month). When prices are high, your fixed amount buys fewer shares; when prices are low, it buys more. Over time, your average cost per share is lower than the average price, because you automatically bought more during dips. This removes the need to time the market and reduces emotional stress.
How to Set It Up
Determine your monthly investment amount (what you can comfortably set aside after essential expenses).
Choose one or two well-diversified instruments: a balanced unit trust, or 2–3 blue-chip stocks you intend to hold for years.
Set up a standing order from your bank account to your brokerage or fund account on a fixed date each month.
Instruct your broker to buy the specified shares or fund units as soon as the funds arrive.
Even TZS 100,000 per month will compound into substantial wealth over decades.

The Psychological Benefit Monthly investing removes constant decision-making. You are no longer asking, “Is now a good time to buy?” because the answer is always yes — you’re investing for 2035, not next week. The Daily DSE Wrap becomes a source of information and comfort, not a trigger for action.
Further Reading: Master the art of reading the Daily Wrap with .
APPLY IT NOW If you haven’t already, set up a standing order this week for a monthly investment amount. Start small if necessary. The habit itself is more important than the amount.

title: Article 7.1 - How to Read the Daily DSE Wrap Like an Analyst
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series

Our Daily DSE Wrap is the heartbeat of this website. Every trading day it delivers a market snapshot, top movers, an “In Focus” story, and a multi-year framework. Reading it as a list of numbers is not enough — you need to extract actionable insight. This guide shows you how.
Step 1: Market Snapshot — What is the regime? The snapshot gives you DSEI, TSI, equity turnover, and deals. Ask yourself:
Is turnover above TZS 3 billion? If yes, the market is liquid and active. Below TZS 1.5 billion, large orders can push prices around.
Are deals high relative to volume? Many deals with moderate volume = broad retail participation. Few deals with massive volume = institutional block trades.
Which index is leading? DSEI up strongly with TSI lagging suggests cross-listed stocks (KCB, NMG) are driving gains.

Step 2: Foreign Flow — The mood of international capital The Wrap gives foreign buying and selling percentages. Low foreign selling (below 5%) indicates the foreign exit is over and local demand is in control. A sudden jump in foreign buying to 15%+ may signal international investors turning positive.

Step 3: Top Movers — Where is the action? Don’t just look at percentage changes; cross-check with volume. A small stock rising 10% on a handful of shares is noise. A large-cap like CRDB or NMB moving 2–3% on high volume is significant.

Step 4: In Focus — The day’s story Here we zoom in on one company or sector. Pay attention to the why — earnings, block trades, regulatory news. This teaches fundamental reasoning you can apply to your own watchlist.

Step 5: Considerations for a Multi-Year Framework — The long view This section is deliberately not a recommendation. It asks: what would a patient, 5-year allocator think? Use it to check your own long-term thesis. If the day’s data conflicts, do more research. If it confirms, you have no reason to act.
Step 6: Today’s Concept — A daily lesson Don’t skip this. Over a year you’ll accumulate 250+ mini-lessons on block trades, inflation-linked bonds, and more.
Further Reading: Protect your capital by understanding liquidity: .

Apply It Now Open today’s Daily Wrap. Using the steps above, answer: (1) What regime is the market in? (2) Which stock move was most meaningful and why? (3) Does the “Considerations” section challenge or support your current portfolio allocation? Write your answers in a notebook.

title: Article 7.2 - Navigating DSE Liquidity: Small-Cap and Illiquid Stocks
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series

Liquidity is the ability to buy or sell an asset quickly without significantly moving the price. On the DSE, liquidity varies dramatically. CRDB and NMB often trade hundreds of thousands of shares daily; some counters trade only a few hundred in a week. Ignoring liquidity is one of the fastest ways to lose money.
Understanding DSE Liquidity Tiers
High (blue-chip): CRDB, NMB, TBL, VODA, KCB. You can usually trade a few hundred shares near the quoted price. Spreads are narrow.
Moderate: TCC, TPCC, TOL, SWIS, NICO. You may need patience; orders of more than 50–100 shares could move the price.
Low (small-cap): DCB, MUCOBA, MBP, PAL, TTP. A single order of a few thousand shillings can change the price by several percent. Spreads are wide.

The Cost of Illiquidity: The Bid-Ask Spread The spread is the gap between what buyers will pay (bid) and what sellers ask (offer). On liquid stocks, it might be 0.1–0.5%; on illiquid stocks, 5–10% or more. If you buy at the offer and immediately sell at the bid, you lose the spread. You must overcome this built-in loss before making a profit.
Practical Rules for Illiquid Counters
Use limit orders only. Never place a market order on a low-volume stock.
Trade smaller sizes. If daily volume is 2,000 shares, don’t try to buy 5,000 in one go.
Be patient. Your order may remain unfilled for days or weeks. That’s normal.
Check outstanding bids and offers. If there are zero bids, do not buy — you won’t be able to sell later without taking a loss.

Liquidity and Valuation Illiquid stocks often appear cheap on P/E or P/B, but that “discount” is the market charging you for illiquidity. Don’t mistake a low price for a bargain. Always ask: If I needed to sell tomorrow, could I do so without losing a large chunk of my capital?

title: Article 7.3 - Case Study: CRDB Bank – A Fundamental Walk-Through
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
This article applies the complete Amana Capital EA Stock Analysis Checklist (Article 4.7) to CRDB Bank Plc. Use it as a model for analysing any holding.

1. Macro Context As of June 2026, Tanzania’s economy is growing at 5–6%, inflation is moderate, and the BOT policy rate is stable. This supports banking: loan demand is rising, credit costs manageable, and the exchange rate relatively stable. Favourable for well-capitalised banks.

2. Industry Analysis Banking is the largest DSE sector. It benefits from financial inclusion, mobile banking, and an expanding middle class. Competitors include NMB, KCB, and smaller domestic banks. CRDB has the largest branch network (a competitive moat), though NMB has a strong mobile and retail franchise.
3. Business Model & Moat Traditional commercial banking: taking deposits and lending. Moats: wide branch network, decades-old brand, high switching costs for corporate clients.
4. Financial Health (illustrative)
Revenue growth: low double-digit, driven by loan book expansion and fee income.
NIM: ~6%, consistent with sector average.
NPLs: ~4.5%, within BOT comfort range.
CAR well above regulatory minimum.
Dividend: regular, moderate payout ratio; management retains earnings for growth.
5. Management Quality Steady, conservative strategy. Clear communication in annual reports. No significant governance red flags. Diversified loan book.
6. Valuation (illustrative)
Share price: TZS 2,590
Book value per share: ~TZS 1,450 → P/B 1.79

Dividend yield: ~3.5% Compared to regional peer KCB (P/B ~1.0–1.2 on NSE), CRDB appears slightly more expensive on a book basis, but its higher ROE and growth potential may justify the premium. Historically, CRDB’s P/E has ranged 7–14, so 10.4 is in the middle — not a deep bargain, but not overvalued.
7. Technical Snapshot Gradual uptrend since early 2026, support around 2,500, resistance near 2,650. Volume on up days healthy, suggesting accumulation.

8. Conclusion CRDB presents a strong, moated bank with solid financials, capable management, and a reasonable valuation — but not a deep-value opportunity. A long-horizon investor might hold or accumulate on dips near 2,500, while a disciplined seller might trim if P/E exceeds 14 without a corresponding earnings increase.

title: Article 7.4 - Corporate Actions Beyond Dividends: Rights Issues, Splits, and Bonus Shares
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series
Dividends are the most common corporate action, but companies also use other mechanisms that affect your holdings. Understanding them is critical.

Rights Issue A company offers existing shareholders the right to buy additional shares at a discount to the market price, in proportion to their current holding (e.g., 1 new share for every 5 held). This raises fresh capital for the company. If you participate, you maintain your ownership percentage and buy at a discount. If you do not, your ownership is diluted (your percentage of the company shrinks), and the value of your existing shares may fall because more shares now exist. Rights are usually renounceable — you can sell your rights in the market if you choose not to take up the offer.

Bonus Issue (Scrip Issue) Also called a capitalisation issue, a bonus issue gives existing shareholders additional shares for free, in proportion to their holding (e.g., 1 bonus share for every 10 held). No new capital is raised; the company simply converts reserves (retained earnings) into share capital. Your ownership percentage does not change, and the share price typically adjusts downward to reflect the increased number of shares, leaving the total value of your holding unchanged. However, bonus issues can improve liquidity and signal management confidence.

Share Split A company divides its existing shares into multiple shares (e.g., a 2-for-1 split doubles the number of shares and halves the price). The total value of your holding stays the same. Splits are often done to make shares more affordable and improve liquidity, which can attract more investors.

Mergers and Acquisitions When two companies combine, shareholders of the acquired company may receive cash, shares in the acquiring company, or a mix. These events can create or destroy value depending on the terms. Always evaluate whether the offer fairly values your shares.
What to Do When a Corporate Action Is Announced
Read the circular from the company carefully.
Understand the impact on your ownership and the share price.
For rights issues, decide whether to participate, sell your rights, or do nothing (the last option usually leads to dilution).
Check the record date — you must hold shares before this date to be entitled.
If uncertain, consult your broker or a financial advisor.

title: Article 7.5 - Green Bonds and ESG Investing on the DSE
content: By Amana Capital East Africa – Investor Education Series

Global capital is shifting toward sustainable investing, and Tanzania is part of this movement. ESG stands for Environmental, Social, and Governance — factors that measure a company’s sustainability and ethical impact. On the DSE, this trend is visible through green bonds and growing governance expectations.

What Are Green Bonds? Green bonds are debt instruments where the proceeds are exclusively used for environmentally beneficial projects — solar farms, reforestation, clean water, sustainable agriculture. The Tanzanian government and some corporations have begun issuing green and sustainable bonds. The DSE lists outstanding sustainable bonds with a face value of over TZS 531 billion (as of June 2026). The Government of Tanzania has also issued infrastructure bonds that may have green components.
Why ESG Matters for Investors
Risk management: Companies with poor governance (corruption, opaque accounting) or environmental liabilities (pollution) face fines, reputational damage, and higher costs.
Return potential: Sustainable companies may be better positioned for long-term growth as regulations tighten and consumer preferences shift.
Access to capital: International investors increasingly demand ESG compliance. DSE companies that adopt strong ESG practices may attract more foreign capital, potentially boosting their share prices.
ESG on the DSE While formal ESG ratings are not yet widespread in Tanzania, you can assess companies yourself:
Environmental: Does the company have a policy on emissions, waste, water usage? Does it face environmental litigation?
Social: How does it treat employees, communities, and customers? Does it promote financial inclusion?
Governance: Is the board independent? Are there conflicts of interest? Is executive pay reasonable and transparent?
Look for disclosures in annual reports and sustainability reports. Companies with strong governance — such as those with diverse, independent boards and clear audit trails — are generally lower risk.

Green Bond Investing Green bonds trade on the DSE like conventional bonds. They offer fixed coupons and may provide an additional feel-good factor, but you should still assess them based on yield, credit risk, and liquidity. The sustainable bond market in Tanzania is growing, and being aware of it positions you at the forefront of a global trend. In our Daily DSE Wrap, we will increasingly highlight sustainable bond trades and ESG-related corporate disclosures as they become available, helping you track this evolving market.
